title: Race, time, and utopia: critical theory and the process of emancipation [Book Review]
author: Battaglini

opening_move.description: The review opens by presenting Paris's central claim: utopia functions as a critical theory in Black political thought to break with racialized temporal domination and inaugurate new social possibilities.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Diagnosing and conceptualising racial injustice as temporal domination in order to formulate emancipatory resources

rivals_engaged: pure structural accounts of racial justice
theoretical_positioning.description: The review is appreciative and expository, presenting Paris's two innovations (utopia as 'not-yet'; racial injustice as temporal domination) and contextualising their theoretical contributions.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: close reading of Paris and critical theory tradition; Du Bois exegesis
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre; largely expository. The normative stake belongs to the reviewed book's argument (utopian emancipation from racial domination).

case_type.case_name: Paris, Race, Time, and Utopia (2025); Du Bois's work

referenced_only: critical theory tradition (Bloch, Frankfurt School implied)

confidence_notes: Book review genre. High normative stake reflects the book's project, not an independent reviewer intervention. The review is primarily expository.
